skill.name: azure-servicebus-dotnet
skill.description: Guidance for using Azure.Messaging.ServiceBus in .NET applications, including clients, queues, topics, subscriptions, sessions, dead-letter queues, and processors.
skill.summary: Build Azure Service Bus messaging workflows in .NET with practical SDK patterns.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer reported many command execution, weak cryptography, reconnaissance, and hardcoded URL patterns. Manual review found these are Markdown examples, identifier matches, and reference links rather than executable behavior. No malicious intent or prompt injection was found; the only real concern is that some sample logging patterns could expose message bodies or exception details if copied into production.

low_findings.title: Sample Logging May Expose Message Data
low_findings.description: The examples print message bodies, dead-letter descriptions, and exception details to the console. This is educational sample code, but production systems should redact sensitive payloads and avoid logging secrets from messages or errors.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines directly show console logging of payload and error values. The issue is low severity because the file is documentation, not code that runs automatically.

low_findings.title: Static External Command Findings Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The command execution detections are caused by Markdown fenced code blocks and inline code examples, including dotnet package installation commands and SDK type names. The skill contains no script file, Ruby code, or automatic execution path.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line-numbered review confirms these are Markdown examples and tables. No evidence found of executable Ruby backticks or generated shell commands.

low_findings.title: Static Weak Cryptography Findings Are Identifier False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography detections are triggered by words such as description and DeadLetterErrorDescription. The skill does not recommend DES, MD5, SHA1, or any cryptographic operation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain metadata and Azure Service Bus dead-letter property names. No cryptographic API or algorithm usage is present in the reviewed file.

low_findings.title: Static Network And Reconnaissance Findings Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs are public reference links to NuGet, Microsoft Learn, and Azure SDK GitHub pages. The reconnaissance hit is a dead-letter queue example, not host or system discovery.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible documentation references and are not used by code. The cited dead-letter line has no system command, filesystem access, or environment inspection.

content.value_statement: Azure Service Bus messaging can be complex to implement correctly in .NET. This skill provides focused SDK patterns for sending, receiving, settlement, sessions, dead-letter handling, and administration.
actual_capabilities: Shows package installation for Azure.Messaging.ServiceBus and Azure.Identity.
Explains ServiceBusClient setup with Microsoft Entra ID and connection strings.
Demonstrates sending, receiving, batching, message settlement, and processors.
Covers sessions, dead-letter queues, topics, subscriptions, and transactions.
Includes administration examples for queues, topics, and subscriptions.
Lists key SDK types, best practices, error handling, and reference links.
limitations: It is documentation only and does not run Azure commands by itself.
Examples use placeholders and require a real Azure namespace and entities.
It does not provision Azure resources outside the shown SDK examples.
The stated SDK version should be verified before production upgrades.

use_cases.title: Add Queue Messaging To A .NET Service
use_cases.description: Use the send, receive, batching, and settlement examples to add reliable queue processing.

use_cases.title: Design Pub Sub Processing With Topics
use_cases.description: Use the topic and subscription guidance to route events to multiple consumers.

use_cases.title: Troubleshoot Dead Letter And Session Workflows
use_cases.description: Use the dead-letter, session, and error handling sections to diagnose processing failures.

prompt_templates.title: Create A Basic Queue Client
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to show me how to create an Azure Service Bus client, sender, and receiver for a .NET queue.

prompt_templates.title: Build A Background Processor
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to design a ServiceBusProcessor workflow with manual completion, error handling, and safe concurrency settings.

prompt_templates.title: Handle Dead Letter Messages
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to explain how my .NET service should read, inspect, and safely reprocess Azure Service Bus dead-letter messages.

prompt_templates.title: Design An Ordered Transactional Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to design an Azure Service Bus .NET flow that uses sessions, cross-entity transactions, and topic subscriptions.

output_examples.input: How should I receive and complete messages from a queue?
output_examples.output: The answer explains creating a receiver, reading one or more messages, processing each body, and completing only after successful work.

output_examples.input: What should I use for ordered processing?
output_examples.output: The answer recommends sessions, explains setting a session identifier, and describes receiving from the next available or a specific session.

output_examples.input: How do I handle failed messages?
output_examples.output: The answer outlines dead-letter settlement, reading from the dead-letter subqueue, checking reason metadata, and avoiding sensitive logs.

best_practices: Prefer DefaultAzureCredential and managed identity for production authentication.
Use processors for background work and configure concurrency intentionally.
Redact message payloads and exception details before writing production logs.
anti_patterns: Do not use connection strings as the default production authentication method.
Do not complete messages before the required processing succeeds.
Do not assume batching succeeds without checking TryAddMessage results.

faq.question: Does this skill execute Azure Service Bus commands?
faq.answer: No. It provides Markdown guidance and SDK examples for developers to adapt.

faq.question: Which .NET package does it focus on?
faq.answer: It focuses on Azure.Messaging.ServiceBus and also references Azure.Identity for authentication.

faq.question: Can it help with topics and subscriptions?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes examples for sending to topics and receiving from subscriptions.

faq.question: Does it cover dead-letter queues?
faq.answer: Yes. It shows how to dead-letter messages and read from the dead-letter subqueue.

faq.question: Does it recommend connection strings?
faq.answer: It shows connection strings as an option, but recommends Microsoft Entra ID for production.

faq.question: Is it suitable for production code without changes?
faq.answer: No. Treat the examples as starting points and add security, logging, retries, and configuration controls.
